species: GHO x2 + Coyote -- Night Predator Chorus
scientific_name: Bubo virginianus (Great Horned Owl) x2 + Canis latrans (Coyote)
sound_type: Night predator chorus: (1) GHO male 300-350 Hz fundamental, 5-note sequence 4-6 sec, harmonics to 2000 Hz, 70-80 dB at 100m; (2) GHO female 400-450 Hz fundamental, same sequence structure, alternating with male, duet cycle 15-30 sec; (3) Coyote howl 800-1200 Hz rising, harmonics to 4000 Hz, 2-5 sec, 75-85 dB at 100m, followed by yip sequence 1000-3000 Hz, 3-8 yips. Combined: 200-4000 Hz, 80-90 dB at center of activity. Temporal structure: GHO duet 15-30 sec periodic cycle + aperiodic Coyote response.
ecosystem: Forest edge, Pacific Northwest lowlands and foothills. Winter. Leafless deciduous trees: acoustic transparency enhanced. Prey concentration at edge: deer mice, voles, rabbits, snowshoe hares. Habitat: Snoqualmie Valley edges, Cowlitz Valley, Puget Sound foothills, distributed PNW.
season: Winter (December-February). Peak: January-February. GHO pair-bond reinforcement peak (earliest-breeding large owl in North America). Coyote breeding season (peak mating February). Long nights (14-16 hr darkness at 47-49°N). Acoustic transparency of leafless winter forest edge.
milestone: FIRST CANID VOCALIZATION IN MULTI-SPECIES SIMULTANEOUS SPS EVENT. Coyote prior entries: SPS 208 (solo howl E=5) + SPS 209 (pup yipping E=4). Both solo-species. Degree 328: FIRST CANIDAE MEMBER IN SIMULTANEOUS MULTI-SPECIES ACOUSTIC CONTEXT. GHO prior entries: SPS 135 (single), 136 (pair duet), 299 (territorial), 309 (pair). Degree 328 is fourth GHO entry, first in multi-species context with Canidae. GHO pair duet: minor third interval between male (300-350 Hz) and female (400-450 Hz). Coyote response to GHO calls: documented behavior, possibly related to GHO predation on Coyote pups. Combined E=8: three predators, two families, winter, darkness.

coordinates.description: Distributed: forest edge ecosystems throughout PNW lowlands and foothills. Representative: Snoqualmie Valley edge habitat ~47.5°N 121.9°W, Cowlitz Valley edges, Puget Sound foothill zones.

acoustic_data.gho_male: 300-350 Hz fundamental. 5-note sequence (hoo-hoo-hoooo hoo-hoo). 0.4-0.8 sec per note. Total 4-6 sec. Harmonics to 2000 Hz. 70-80 dB at 100m. Carries 1-2 km in still winter air.
acoustic_data.gho_female: 400-450 Hz fundamental (minor third above male). Same structure. Alternates with male: female initiates or responds within 0.5-2 sec. Brief overlaps at transitions. Duet cycle repeats every 15-30 sec.
acoustic_data.coyote: Howl: 800-1200 Hz rising fundamental, harmonics to 4000 Hz, 2-5 sec, 75-85 dB at 100m. Carries 3-5 km. Yip sequence: 1000-3000 Hz, 0.1-0.3 sec per yip, 3-8 yips. Pack response (if present): 80-90 dB. Triggered by GHO duet.
acoustic_data.combined: 200-4000 Hz. 80-90 dB at center of activity. Temporal: GHO periodic (15-30 sec cycle) + Coyote aperiodic (triggered response). Structurally complex: two periodic sources + one responding source in real-time interaction.
acoustic_data.e8_calibration: E=8 through nocturnal multi-species territorial intensity. Each species alone: GHO solo E=5, GHO pair E=5-6, Coyote solo E=5. Together in winter forest edge with active interaction: E=8. The Coyote's addition to the GHO pair raises the energy by amplifying the territorial exchange across two predator families.

nodes.title: FIRST CANID IN MULTI-SPECIES SIMULTANEOUS SPS EVENT
nodes.description: Coyote (Canis latrans) appears at SPS 208 (solo howl) and 209 (pup yipping). At degree 328: first Canidae member in simultaneous multi-species acoustic context. Active interaction with Great Horned Owl pair. FIRST CANIDAE IN MULTI-SPECIES SPS.

nodes.title: GHO-Coyote Acoustic Interaction -- Cross-Family Territorial Exchange
nodes.description: Great Horned Owls prey on Coyote pups. Adult Coyotes respond to GHO calls, possibly as territorial signal or predator awareness behavior. The practical result: GHO pair duet triggers Coyote howling; Coyote howling can trigger increased GHO calling. The acoustic exchange is cross-species territorial signaling, not aggressive encounter.

nodes.title: Winter Peak Overlap -- Both Species at Maximum Vocal Activity
nodes.description: GHO: earliest-breeding large owl in North America. Pair-bond reinforcement peak January-February. Coyote: breeding season peak February. Both species at maximum territorial vocal activity simultaneously. Long winter nights (14-16 hr). Acoustic transparency of leafless forest edge. Three converging conditions: peak behavior + long nights + transparency.

cross_references.relationship: First GHO entry. Solo territorial. 328 is pair + Coyote.
The isolated duet. 328 is duet plus active Coyote response -- adds Canidae to the acoustic event.
Solo Coyote E=5. Degree 328: FIRST CANID IN MULTI-SPECIES EVENT. Same vocalization, new context.
Solo Coyote family group E=4. 328 documents adult Coyote in cross-species interaction.
Other Canidae. Wolf in wilderness. Coyote at forest edge. Different habitats, same family documented at 328.
GHO pair in earlier return at E=7. 328: pair + Coyote = E=8. The Coyote adds one energy unit.

E=8 + E=8 = E=16. MAXIMUM COMBINED ENERGY IN 325-328 SEQUENCE. Nocturnal predator aggression: ecological (winter forest edge) vs. cultural (heavy punk grief and rage). FIRST CANID + MOST INTENSE FEMALE-LED HEAVY BAND in the engine at the same degree.
